text: The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the liver, small bowel, and colon. The sigmoid colon is released from congenital adhesions and peritoneal attachments to the lateral pelvic wall, ensuring complete mobilization for a tension-free extracorporeal anastomosis.

The surgeon has completely freed the sigmoid colon from its lateral attachments, revealing the gonadal vessels, iliac vessels, iliopsoas muscle, and left ureter covered by retroperitoneal fascia. The incision on the lateral peritoneum is extended cranially from the sigmoid colon to the descending colon up to the splenic flexure, with the peritoneal fold incised but no lateral to medial dissection performed.

The surgeon displaces the omentum upwards into the supracolic compartment and lifts the splenic flexure of the colon, creating a tenting effect that highlights the left colic vessels. The peritoneum above and below these vessels is incised, allowing entry into the loose areolar tissue between the retroperitoneal fascia and the colonic mesentery. The white line of Toldt is visible, guiding the dissection in this avascular space, which is developed using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon continues the lateral dissection at the splenic flexure, revealing the bluish tinge of the lower polar spleen beneath the peritoneum. The median to lateral dissection progresses at the descending colon, pushing the retroperitoneal fascia downward to connect with the previously incised lateral peritoneum.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum of the transverse mesocolon and proceeds with cranial dissection, encountering the body and tail of the pancreas. Care is taken to stay anterior to the pancreas, avoiding the retro pancreatic plane to prevent injury to the splenic and renal veins. The transverse mesocolon attachment to the inferior border of the pancreas is divided, allowing entry into the lesser sac.

The surgeon frees the peritoneal attachment on the anterior surface of the inferior border of the pancreas and divides the retroperitoneal attachment of the splenic flexure. Care is taken to avoid injury to the tail of the pancreas, which may be lifted during traction. The medial to lateral dissection is completed, and the left colic vessels are cleared of peritoneum and fibro fatty tissue, then doubly clipped and divided at their origin.

The surgeon clips and divides a proximally arising first sigmoid branch from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), aiding in the mobilization of the descending colon specimen. The linocolic ligament is divided, and the lateral and medial planes of dissection are joined, completing the separation of the splenic flexure and descending colon from the retroperitoneal fascia, pancreas, and lower pole of the spleen.

The transverse colon is dissected free from its omental attachments from the mid transverse colon to the splenic flexure. The left colon is fully mobilized, revealing a tumor at the splenic flexure with a large umbilication on its surface. The specimen is delivered through a midline wound using a wound protector, and a stapled anastomosis is performed extracorporeally. The distal segment of the colon is delivered into the abdominal wound following adequate lateral sigmoid mobilization.
